speaker: interviewee
text: --if you can try that, come [BACKROUND NOISE] and, please, just help us when they enter--

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --to enter so quietly that nobody will hear them. Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. There's kids--

speaker: interviewee
text: Bye-bye.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: P-E-L-Y?

speaker: interviewer
text: P-E-L-L-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure there's a-- one-- --Bem's name. OK. And if you identify now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 3, Esther Bem. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, much later in life, I realized that I was one of those hidden children that had to live in plain sight. The only thing I ever had to hide was my identity and my Jewishness. And there were moments in those days that I was-- I wished secretly that they would find us, put us on a train, and that finally I could be myself, tell the truth who I am, open up, and not live in this constant anxiety. There was-- fear was a constant companion. It was an emotion that was always with us. I never-- because I felt a tremendous responsibility because I was the spokesman all the time. And I had to grow up so fast and become an adult, behave like an adult and behave like-- be a responsible person, although I looked like a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you understand what the consequences would have been if you were found?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. We knew that if we are caught by the Germans, this is the end. I mean, we, we either got executed right away, shot, or put on a train or going-- [LAUGHS] we knew already where we are supposed to go and where we are supposed to be as Jews. And another thing was we felt responsible for those people that were shielding us, like this priest. When I think back, I mean, this was a tremendous responsibility and tremendous thing from a, a, a man to trust a child not to speak. Because don't forget, these are years when you-- secrecy for an adolescent is a very hard thing to live with. I mean, I was sometimes tempted. Oh, I have to find somebody to confide. Of course, I didn't. [LAUGHS] And the best thing for me was just not to go anywhere. I lived in total isolation in my own head most of the time, dreaming about food, about times that are passed. And very often, I thought that I'm going to make something really terrific out of my life if I come out of it, which I didn't. [LAUGHS] I did, and I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the priest's name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I only know that I waited for him with such anticipation and such-- I loved him. He came with books always. I begged him to bring me books, and he did. The books were always about saints [LAUGHS] and miracles and Christianity. But I really didn't care. It was printed word. It was arousing my imagination. And the only thought that somebody who knows who we are will see us every few hours-- every few days was enough to live for those few hours that he came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you learning about the Catholic religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. He once asked me to participate in a seminar for young girls because he had to show to the village that I am Christian. And I did. It was a few days of [SIGHS] total revelation to me about the Christian religion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- in some way, I was afraid that I may be-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not falling in love with it, far from it. But it did fascinate me. And I think my mother was right, when I think back. I could have been, maybe, seduced by it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because it was safe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. Now, one day, he told me that my girlfriends are around and that they are in a monastery. And he said, you know something? I will try let you go and visit them.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are your friends from where?

speaker: interviewee
text: From that village where we lived openly as Jews, as-- and I walked, I think, three, four hours to that monastery when he allowed me to go and visit them. And I think one of the reasons was because I was so terribly lonely. And he wanted me to have one day of something. And secondly, maybe he was hoping that I would be seduced by the life in a-- the monastery. And they converted. My girlfriends converted. And their families did. And they had a much better life than we did. They were much more affluent than we did. They had more food and better life. And my girlfriends in that monastery with the nuns, they went to school. They had some disciplined life. And they had a nice life. And I thought to myself some of those moments, looking at them, I wish I was with you. But I had to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in that village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] now, I'm going to tell you about the most crucial moment of my survival. And I don't want anybody to think that this was some heroics. It wasn't. It was mainly due to a big dose of good luck and a good moment, as every survivor, I think, had these combination of moments. And this was my moment. And I'm going to talk to you about it. One day, the priest came. He was very pensive and very grave. And he said, look, people are gossiping too much in this town. They're questioning why don't you have ration cards because you never go to the general store to shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is impossible. In those days in Italy, not a soul could live without ration cards. You have to be registered in a municipality. And you have to do it right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you know your story. You go there and tell them. You go with your father, with Arturo. You talk. You are the spokesman. You definitely continue telling them that he is still under the shock of the bombing. He cannot speak. Don't let them ambush you. He was briefing me what I'm going to say. Be very careful when they ask you. Think. Answer only what they ask you, and you'll see, you'll be fine. And I still don't believe it when I look back, as a grown woman, that he had trusted me. Because I think if they found out, we would have been hanging on that square with him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Next morning, my father and I ventured on this mission. I was excited. I thought to myself, finally, something's happening to me, some excitement. I felt I was almost like a debutant. I'm going into this City Hall that I always looked from outside. Once in awhile, I would get there and fast go home. And that was the first time that we walked, actually, in the street, my father and I. We entered that City Hall, and a clerk was sitting in the front. I went fast to him and started my story, thinking, well, if I tell him fast, I'll get it over fast. He stopped me. He said, wait. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I looked around me. Adolf Hitler is framed in every position on the walls, swastikas all over. My father was white like a ghost. I knew that I cannot even meet his eyes, because if I do, I'll lose it. He was shaking. The clerk came out. I think you can go through something like this only when you are young and totally innocent and stupid. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The clerk came out. [SIGHS] He said, follow me. And there was this beautiful door. And it looked to me majestic. And then we entered that room. He closes the door behind us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the worst nightmare was sitting on the throne-- an SS officer with-- in a very elegant uniform. [BACKROUND NOISE] I don't know what kind of rank he had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't introduce himself to us. [LAUGHS] And he was looking at us, like, with these piercing eyes. And I think the fear of uniform I really never shed off. I-- even today, when a policeman stops me-- the most benevolent policeman-- I shake. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, of course, as befitted the master race, was even good-looking. He was quite young. And behind him was a man standing in civil clothes, which I didn't know at that moment who he was. But then I realized he was the interpreter who was supposed to interpret from German to Italian and back and forth. And I spoke quite well German. As you know, my maternal grandmother was Austrian. And I made right away in my brain, like, a decision that I don't know German because German-- only refugees spoke and Jews. An Italian-- a pure Italian speaks only Italian. And he started questioning. The questions were long, [SIGHS] very precise. And I always waited for the interpreter. And then I answered. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Suddenly, in one moment, he said, ask her if she speaks German. It was to confuse me. I waited, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] answered no. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And as I told you, I think that every one of us here that is alive had this one moment that either you went to the wrong destination or you were saved. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. I still cannot figure it out, how a fanatic, well-trained officer could believe me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking at us, how could he not see who we are? My father had this, this traumatic experience. I mean, this was all unreal.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We walked out [SIGHS] with the cards-- with the ration cards. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know one thing-- I was thinking about it a lot, very often in my life. One of the possibilities was that he did not want to disrupt his peaceful existence in that village. Don't forget, this was already 1944. This was spring of 1944. The Germans were losing the war. He knew exactly what was going on the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he thought, now he's in a wonderful place, that he will survive the war. Why does he have to disrupt it? Why does he have to hang us there on that place so that the whole village will be against him for the next eight months? Or maybe, maybe there was some humanity in him. Who knows? Or maybe I really fooled him. I mean, [LAUGHS] I don't know. I will probably never know. Of course I will never know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I grew up. That was a moment when I really knew I'm grown-up, after that incident. And now, [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we lived in that village. We were very poor. And I didn't tell you something, which I'm going to tell you fast. I learned to weave baskets, still in that first village.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now we were pretty desperate for food. And I started to weave those baskets again. I was afraid before because I thought, what if somebody from that village finds out this was one thing I was doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it became like an assembly line. My parents were cutting it for me and dipping it in cold water. I was doing it. And I was going around and exchanging it for bread or, or a butter or a piece of cheese or some grapes. And that's how we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the townspeople think about the fact that you didn't go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I always found different excuses, that this is not a good school for me, that I don't know anybody. Listen, I did not want to interact with anybody. And I didn't. I was always-- they may have thought that I may be adult. Whatever they wanted, they thought. But here we were legal now. We had our cards. We had-- we had our names. Whatever the gossip behind our backs was not important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the priest ever come to you again and say that you needed to do anything else to be more legitimate?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We went to church every Sunday. We tried to go in the-- like, the church would start either at-- the first mass would be at 6:00, and the second at 7:00, and

speaker: interviewee
text: 00. So every time we went to a different mass so that different people would see us because, of course, as not being baptized, we couldn't take the Holy Ghost. And they stood in line every Sunday to take the Holy Ghost. We couldn't. So we were always trying to hide between this guy and that guy. And it was horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: It-- every Sunday was an anxiety attack for the three of us. Already, two days before Sunday, we were so depressed and, and afraid. What if somebody starts questioning, why aren't you taking this Holy Ghost?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As I told you, these were days-- the most macabre days for me, loneliness, nothing to do. The only refuge for me was to go to sleep because these were a few hours that I didn't know anything, that I didn't-- wasn't bored. I was longing for a girlfriend or to see my girlfriends again. The isolation was tough. And the lack of identity or to do what every teenager does was very, very hard. But you do what you have to do. You survive. And the name of the game was the survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your typical day like? What would you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was writing a, a romance. [LAUGHS] I still have it. And the way it was written, it was in such an Italian that I probably wouldn't understand half of it today. It was the language of the-- scholarly language. That's how I wrote because that's what I read-- those type of books I read. And that gave me a lot of pleasure. And it was-- I was-- in that book, in that story, I was describing a boy who lives in a castle-- that was very interesting-- who is blind. Because I felt like this. I felt like I'm blind. I don't-- cannot see anything out of this place where I am. And I'm in a-- in a castle. I'm never out of that castle. I'm totally isolated. And this was the story. I was putting myself into his personality. Then I was weaving those baskets and trying to sell them. I was helping the farmers when they-- when the time was for the grapes. I don't know how you say it in-- when the grapes were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Harvest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. At a time of the harvest, I was helping them and, of course, getting some fruit and taking it home. And when I would go, let's say, to a place where the harvest was, and I would get acquainted with somebody, right away, I run away and never came back because I didn't want to be questioned, who we are and what we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I read a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know news from the outside? Did you know what was going on with the rest of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were hermetically isolated. We had no clue what was going on the front. We had nowhere to, to read, only propaganda from the newspaper. We did not have money to buy the newspaper-- if we would see it somewhere. We had no clue how close the end was. And I have to tell you that when the front passed through our village already-- this was 1945-- and the Germans were retreating in rags, we were still hiding. We still did not get out. Until one day-- and here I have to say that probably half of the village must have known that we are Jewish. But they were not telling each other. They were such noble people. Because when, one day, an armored, big truck stopped in front of the house where we lived, and Palestinian young men jumped out of that armored car with Magen David here, they were looking for Jews. Probably somebody showed them where we are. And that's how they liberated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the moment you realized the war was ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was that end.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was May, 1945, for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened in May, 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1945, in May, when they came, it looked to me like a Fata Morgana or whatever, having-- seeing somebody with the [LAUGHS] Magen David. And here we were two years living in a different identity. And for me, the transition to freedom was very hard. I could not get back to my own identity for a long time. For the longest time, I was still Tamino. I still had in my mind that I was the refugee. When I went back to my country, to Yugoslavia, and went back to school, even then I had my moments. I would wake up in the morning. I would still think I'm Elsa Tamino.

speaker: interviewer
text: The transition was tough. Why? Probably because I wanted to ingrain this so deep in my heart who, who I was not, not to ever let it know to anybody, that the transition to the real world was tough. How long did you stay in Italy after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Palestinians came, they-- in a few hours, we were on that truck going to Venice. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Venice, they took us to hotel, which was called Hotel Internazionale, I remember. And there was many refugees there that they found. My friends were there. My friends were there. My-- all the-- their parents went there. And we stayed in Venice until we found out about my sisters, who is alive, who is not. Because if they wouldn't have been alive, we wouldn't have gone back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: So my parents, through Red Cross, got a notice that my younger sister, who is nine years older than me, that she is alive. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yugoslavia. In the-- she was still in the army. She was a major in the Partisans Army. She had-- She was ordained for bravery. And we didn't know anything about the other. But my parents still hoped. They said, well, we didn't find out. But she's probably somewhere. And after about four months, we went back. I-- we came back in about October.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the-- to Zagreb, where we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have anymore this apartment, no. We found some other apartment. This was taken already by some partisan officer. And we wouldn't have wanted to live there anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Furniture was all gone. There was nothing left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody when you got back to Zagreb?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. I went back to-- I went to school. It was a special school for children that lost all these years of schooling. And there were many of them that I knew from before. Some I didn't. But I felt very comfortable with this group of people because they were children that grew up like me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was a mixed school. Now, with people-- with contemporaries that had-- that went through nothing, that lived a normal life, I felt extremely not fitting. And I didn't feel right with them. But I never shared my experiences with them because I didn't want to be even more different. And with the friends that went through similar or-- similar situations as I did, in different form, we felt some kind of a kinship. But we never shared our experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, I just recently heard what Elie Wiesel said, that 10 years after the Holocaust, he was mainly silent. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was silent all these years, thinking that nobody's going to believe me anyway. So why talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I felt that I'm growing old, that I'm getting to sunset of my life and, and maybe people that really-- the survivors, there will be so few that have first-hand experiences, then I felt this need, tremendous need, to share it. And I started to pull out of the storage of my memory things that I-- that were hidden far-- somewhere I didn't want to know about it. And after the war, also, only in the '70s I started to read, very, very carefully and very seldom, Elie Wiesel, Primo Levi, Jerzy Kosinski later. But I didn't want to get involved. I didn't want to be obsessed with it. And when I found out that Primo Levi committed suicide, it scared the hell out of me. And I felt that memories can be a very dangerous preoccupation. And again, I postponed. I postponed for a later date. I knew it will come. But I knew the time wasn't right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly not because I was clever or smart or better than anybody else. And-- because it was a chance, pure chance, the-- a moment that probably this officer either lost his mind or whatever it took before. And I must say that I had guilt. And I always will have, thinking of all the people that didn't survive. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think some were meant--

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kept you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] During those days, I think [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just the need to survive. You do what you have to do. The alternative was worse. And as I told you, maybe I shouldn't have because I was one of those that had moments that I felt guilty that I should now be found out-- enough, enough of this fear. I survived mainly because we were surrounded with good people, people that made a choice to help, that could have chosen not to, but they did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Personally, I don't want to take any credit for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever gone back and seen any of those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I did once with my husband Viktor. I did not go to that village where we were hiding. I just couldn't make my-- it was in my intention. But I couldn't, just emotionally couldn't. I wasn't right for it. I went back to that first village, Possagno, which was a very emotional experience. And I knew I can't take more of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So yes, I, I met a few people that I was friends with in those days. They were old already. But yes, I did go back. And how long did you go-- did you live in Yugoslavia after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived five years. And then I went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you got to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I married. I had children. I worked. I did not have the opportunity to go to school anymore. But it was a pioneering life, and nice and free, and very good to be young in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about who--

speaker: interviewer
text: Three.

speaker: interviewer
text: --who you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I married a few-- a year after I arrived to Israel. My husband was a survivor from the Holocaust, like I was. I met him the first time right after the war, when I was 15. And then we didn't see each other until we-- we were not connected at all. And we came to Israel. We fell in love. We married. We had our children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mirko.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your children. When were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was born in 1954. Her name is Liora. And my son was born in 1962. His name is Amir.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you live in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived 16 years in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I would like to, [SIGHS] I think, conclude [SIGHS] this meeting. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I would like that post-Holocaust generation [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to know that there was decency and goodness in those days, that not everything was negative. I think, in my case, I can thank these people-- not only the whole Italian population, which, of course, I do-- but to people that really knew that they will have no reward, no gratification from us-- from us, people that sacrificed their life, their security, future, maybe, of their families because if found out, they would have been executed. This was in times when the Nazi machinery-- death machinery-- was functioning beautifully. It was oiled well. They made a choice to, to save us, to shield us, to feed us when they didn't have anything by themselves. They were people totally altruistic. They did it without making us feel as intruders in their everyday life. And we were. We were intruding in their peaceful existence. We were intruding on their food. I mean, they had nothing, and they shared it with us. They had no water. They had no electricity. And they knew that if they are found out, that it's, it's their end.
